meta.title: X540 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Jaguar E-Pace X540 engine — D150, D165, D180, D200, P200, P250, P300, P300e. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 15 vetted Jaguar specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar E-Pace X540 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X540 engine typically costs £3,000–£9,500 supply only [EM-QUOTE], depending on engine — the 2.0-litre diesel sits at £3,500–£6,500, while the P300e plug-in hybrid can reach £9,500. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on engine and specialist location. Evaluate the car's value carefully before committing.

mainEntity.name: Which Jaguar E-Pace X540 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The P200 and P250 2.0-litre Ingenium petrols are the most reliable options — they have fewer major failure modes than the diesels, no DPF or oil dilution issues, and a proven architecture. The 1.5-litre three-cylinder is newer, with fewer long-term data points [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar E-Pace X540?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain wear and oil dilution from DPF regeneration on the 2.0-litre diesel Ingenium are the two most significant engine problems — both can lead to premature engine failure. The 2.0-litre petrol Ingenium can suffer from cooling system leaks, which are cheap to prevent but expensive if ignored. The P300e PHEV may experience battery degradation over time.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar E-Pace X540 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. Petrol-powered X540s are generally reliable, well-understood designs. The diesel range carries the Ingenium diesel reputation — timing chain and oil dilution risks are the primary concerns. The P300e PHEV is the most complex; early reliability is promising, but the battery is a long-term risk.

mainEntity.name: Which Jaguar E-Pace X540 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is a hard avoid, but the 2.0-litre diesel Ingenium requires careful consideration — timing chain wear and oil dilution are well-documented risks. Without documented chain work or evidence of regular short-interval oil changes, it's a higher-risk purchase. The P300e PHEV's battery replacement cost (£6,500+) is also a significant long-term consideration.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E-Pace engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cold-start rattle on any diesel E-Pace is the classic early warning of timing chain wear — investigate immediately. Oil dilution can be detected by checking the dipstick for a fuel smell. Coolant loss, rough running, or warning lights on any engine are also key warning signs. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar E-Pace engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 E-Pace, a £4,500–£6,500 diesel recon is 30–43% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £7,000 example, the same recon is 64–93% — walk away. Our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold is the line: if replacement cost exceeds 60% of the car's value, evaluate scrap first [EM-VERIFIED].

mainEntity.name: How long do Jaguar E-Pace engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.0-litre Ingenium petrols can exceed 120,000 miles with proper servicing — cooling system maintenance is the key to longevity. The diesel Ingenium is limited by its timing chain and oil dilution risks; addressed proactively with chain work and 6,000-mile oil changes, it can reach 100,000+ miles. The P300e battery is rated for approximately 1,500–2,000 charge cycles before significant degradation.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the pre-facelift and facelift E-Pace?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2020 facelift introduced mild-hybrid technology across most of the range, the 1.5-litre three-cylinder (P160 and P300e), and updated infotainment. The facelift also brought D165 and D200. Costs and reliability profiles are similar, but the MHEV systems add complexity — see our facelift-specific analysis for details.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar E-Pace X540 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block. Tell us your variant (D150, D165, D180, D200, P160, P200, P250, P300, or P300e) and we'll identify it — 15 vetted Jaguar specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Jaguar E-Pace X540 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E-Pace X540 — from the 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel four to the 1.5-litre three-cylinder P300e plug-in hybrid. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for Jaguar's second-ever SUV. Backed by 140+ real UK X540 enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar E-Pace X540 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E-Pace X540 — Ingenium petrols, diesels, mild hybrids, and the P300e plug-in hybrid with its 1.5-litre three-cylinder.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages. The 2.0-litre Ingenium petrols (P200, P250, P300) are generally considered more reliable than the diesels — the AJ200P has fewer major failure modes than the diesel AJ200D. The 1.5-litre three-cylinder (AJ150) is a newer engine, with fewer long-term data points. The diesel range (D150, D165, D180, D200) carries the Ingenium diesel reputation — timing chain and oil dilution risks are the primary concerns. The P300e plug-in hybrid adds battery and electric motor complexity.

overview.intro: The X540 is Jaguar's second production SUV, revealed on 13 July 2017. Built on JLR's PTA platform, it shares its underpinnings with the second-generation Range Rover Evoque. The E-Pace was offered with a range of 2.0-litre Ingenium four-cylinder engines — both petrol and diesel — with a significant 2020 facelift introducing mild-hybrid technology across most of the range, a 1.5-litre three-cylinder petrol, and the brand's first plug-in hybrid in this segment: the P300e.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: JLR PTA (Premium Transverse Architecture) • Predecessor: None (first-generation E-Pace) • Successor: None (current generation, production ended 2024) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (single body style).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The X540 E-Pace generated 140+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], concentrated on the 2.0-litre diesel and petrol variants as the model reaches the used market.

items.quote: The most powerful diesel in the range and the only one with mild-hybrid assistance — smoother stop-start and better urban efficiency than the non-hybrid diesels. The extra 20 bhp over the D180 transforms the E-Pace's real-world response.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy with the latest technology.

items.quote: The sweet spot in the petrol range — more power than the P200, less complexity than the twin-turbo P300, and mild-hybrid assistance for smoother city driving. The most popular petrol in UK enquiries.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want strong performance without the P300's running costs.

items.quote: The Ingenium diesel family carries known risks — timing chain tensioner issues on early examples and oil dilution from DPF regeneration are the primary concerns. The D200's MHEV system adds another layer of complexity.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise fuel economy and are willing to maintain the diesel system carefully.

items.quote: The most complex powertrain in the E-Pace range — a 1.5-litre three-cylinder turbo engine, an 80 kW electric motor on the rear axle, and a 15 kWh battery pack. More components mean more potential failure points, and battery replacement is a £6,000+ proposition.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want 309 bhp and electric-only running for short commutes.

items.quote: The entry-level petrol is the smart buy for most UK buyers — 197 bhp is more than adequate, the MHEV system helps with urban fuel economy, and the engine is simpler than the twin-turbo P300. Lower running costs and proven Ingenium architecture make this the value champion.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement with sensible running costs.

items.quote: The P300e is easily the most overlooked E-Pace — 309 bhp, 540 Nm of torque, 0-62 mph in 6.5 seconds, and up to 34 miles of electric-only range. The three-cylinder engine is smooth, the electric motor adds instant torque, and company car tax bills are dramatically lower than any other E-Pace. It's a genuine performance SUV that also happens to be the most efficient.
items.whoItsFor: company car drivers and buyers who want performance without the fuel bills.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — Ingenium diesel requires careful maintenance.
Solid Choice — newer engine, fewer known issues.
Safe Bet — the most reliable E-Pace engines.
Watch — battery health is key; warranty essential.

economicsRule.title: The X540 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,500–£6,500 diesel recon against a £7,000–£22,000 E-Pace sits at 16–93% of the car's value【EM-VERIFIED】. On a sub-£8,000 example, that brushes our 60% Jaguar scrap threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £15,000+ example, the same recon drops to 23–43% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X540 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: P200 petrol MHEV — simple 2.0-litre Ingenium, no turbo complexity, no DPF issues.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X540 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: P300e PHEV — battery and electric motor add significant long-term cost risk.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X540 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: P250 petrol MHEV — strong performance, MHEV efficiency, and proven Ingenium reliability.

problems.title: Ingenium Diesel Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.rootCause: The timing chain tensioner on the 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel can wear prematurely, leading to chain stretch and eventual failure. Cold-start rattle is the classic early warning sign. The chain is located at the rear of the engine, making replacement labour-intensive.

tieredCosts.work: Timing chain, tensioner and guide replacement

problems.recommendation: At the first sign of cold-start rattle on any diesel E-Pace, get the chain inspected immediately — an £800–£1,800 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £4,500–£7,500 replacement that follows if ignored. On an E-Pace worth under £8,000, this decision alone determines whether the car stays economically viable.

cta.label: Compare Ingenium diesel engine prices for your E-Pace →

problems.title: Ingenium Diesel Oil Dilution from DPF Regeneration
problems.rootCause: During DPF regeneration, unburnt diesel fuel can enter the engine oil, diluting it and reducing its lubricating properties. This is particularly problematic for urban-driven cars that don't complete full regeneration cycles.

tieredCosts.work: Oil and filter change, DPF inspection

problems.recommendation: For urban-driven diesel E-Paces, shorten oil change intervals to 6,000 miles or annually — a £150–£350 oil change is cheap insurance against a £4,500+ engine replacement. Avoid repeated short trips without letting the DPF regeneration complete.

cta.label: Compare Ingenium diesel engine prices for your E-Pace →

problems.title: P300e PHEV Battery Degradation & Electric Motor Issues
problems.rootCause: The 15 kWh lithium-ion battery pack will naturally degrade over time, reducing electric-only range. The 80 kW electric motor on the rear axle adds complexity to the powertrain.
problems.recommendation: Have the PHEV battery health checked annually — a £300–£800 inspection is cheap insurance against a £6,500+ battery replacement. If you're buying a used P300e, get a battery health report before committing. The 34-mile electric range is a major selling point — protect it.

cta.label: Compare P300e battery prices for your E-Pace →

problems.title: 2.0-litre Ingenium Petrol Cooling System Leaks
problems.rootCause: The 2.0-litre Ingenium petrol engines can suffer from coolant leaks from the water pump and cooling system hoses. This is the most common failure on the otherwise robust petrol range.

tieredCosts.work: Full engine replacement if overheating caused head gasket failure

problems.recommendation: Have the cooling system inspected annually and replace the water pump as preventative maintenance at 60,000 miles — a £400–£800 specialist job is trivial against the £4,000+ engine replacement that follows an ignored coolant leak.

cta.label: Compare Ingenium petrol engine prices for your E-Pace →
